title: Thermal bottleneck in a freely suspended superconducting island on InAs nanowire
authors: E. V. Shpagina, E. S. Tikhonov, D. Ruhstorfer, G. Koblmueller, V. S. Khrapai
abstract: We investigate the heat balance in superconducting islands (S-islands) formed in epitaxial Al/InAs nanowires (NWs) freely suspended above the substrate. We employ a Joule spectroscopy approach, which traces the superconductor-normal transition in the S-island mediated by heating of the neighboring InAs NW segments via transport current. The temperature of the surrounding 3He bath is varied with nearby mesoscopic heaters and controlled with the NW Johnson noise thermometry. The experiment reveals a substantial thermal relaxation bottleneck associated with the cooling via surrounding 3He, which gives rise to phonon heating in the S-island. Our results uncover the role of environmental cooling in non-equilibrium experiments in S-islands in NW devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates that superconducting islands on suspended nanowires experience a thermal bottleneck where phonons cannot efficiently cool to the bath temperature. By applying a three-temperature model, the authors show that heat is primarily dissipated through the surrounding 3He environment rather than through the nanowire itself.
main_problem: The study investigates the heat balance and thermal relaxation in superconducting islands on freely suspended InAs nanowires, specifically addressing why the standard two-temperature model fails to describe the observed thermal dynamics.
main_result: The researchers identified a thermal relaxation bottleneck where phonon heating occurs because the S-island's phonon temperature exceeds the bath temperature, a phenomenon best described by a three-temperature model (3TM) involving environmental cooling via a 3He wetting layer.
method: The study uses Joule spectroscopy to trace the superconductor-to-normal transition and Johnson noise thermometry to calibrate temperatures, while using mesoscopic heaters to vary the effective bath temperature.
model_or_system: The system consists of epitaxial Al/InAs nanowires with an Al shell, freely suspended above a substrate and immersed in a 3He environment. The thermal dynamics are analyzed using a Three-Temperature Model (3TM) that accounts for electron, phonon, and environmental temperature gradients.
key_observables: Critical Joule power (Pj_c), differential resistance (R_diff), noise temperature (T_N), and superconducting critical temperature (T_c) as a function of magnetic field.
important_parameters: Al shell thickness (42 nm), InAs core diameter (~150 nm), electron-phonon coupling strength (Sigma_e-ph), and environmental thermal conductance (Sigma_env).
main_assumptions: Assumes the existence of a 3He wetting layer for cooling; assumes the phonon heat flux through the nanowire core is negligible compared to environmental cooling.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device layout, heater arrangement, and I-V curves demonstrating the S-N transition. Supplemental figures include SEM images, magnetic field-dependent resistance plots, and plots showing the non-steady-state behavior of critical power.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the thermal bottleneck problem, describes the experimental platform and Joule spectroscopy method, presents the failure of the 2TM and the success of the 3TM, analyzes the cooling mechanism via 3He, and concludes with a discussion on the wetting layer and non-steady-state effects.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 1. Device layout and impact of heaters. (a): Sketch of thermal pathways in a freely-suspended NW device. Quasi- particles enter the superconductor and heat the electron sys- tem (temperature Te). The electrons cool to the phonon system in the S-island (Tph) and phonons cool to the bath. The effective bath temperature (T ∗ bath) is controlled with the nearby heater. (b): False color scanning electron micrograph with the electrical circuit. H1 and H3 are non-contact heaters; H2 is a contact heater connected to one of the NW terminals. The other NW terminal is connected to a low-temperature amplifier (LTAmp) for noise measurement.
